questions.question: An ambulance siren emits a frequency of $800\text{ Hz}$. The ambulance travels at $30\text{ m/s}$ toward a stationary observer. If the speed of sound is $340\text{ m/s}$, what frequency does the observer hear?
The same ambulance ($800\text{ Hz}$ siren) passes the observer and moves away at $30\text{ m/s}$. What frequency is heard now? ($v = 340\text{ m/s}$)
A stationary train horn emits a frequency of $500\text{ Hz}$. An observer walks toward the train at $5.0\text{ m/s}$. What frequency does the observer hear? ($v = 340\text{ m/s}$)
The same observer walks away from the stationary train at $5.0\text{ m/s}$. What frequency is heard? ($v = 340\text{ m/s}$)
A sound source moves toward a stationary observer. The observed frequency will be:
questions.explanation: When the source approaches, wavefronts compress, wavelength decreases, and the observed frequency increases above the source frequency.

questions.question: A stationary observer hears a frequency of $1,020\text{ Hz}$ from a source that normally emits $900\text{ Hz}$. If the source is moving toward the observer and $v = 340\text{ m/s}$, what is the source's speed?
A train whistle emits $660\text{ Hz}$. As the train moves away at $25\text{ m/s}$, what frequency is heard? ($v = 340\text{ m/s}$)
If the speed of a sound source toward an observer increases, the observed frequency:
questions.explanation: A faster-approaching source compresses wavefronts more, producing an even higher observed frequency.

questions.question: A moving source is heard at $450\text{ Hz}$ when moving away at $20\text{ m/s}$. If $v = 340\text{ m/s}$, what is the actual source frequency?
A source emits $1,000\text{ Hz}$. Both source and observer are stationary. What frequency is observed? ($v = 340\text{ m/s}$)
questions.explanation: With no relative motion, there is no Doppler shift. $f' = f = 1,000\text{ Hz}$.

questions.question: A car's horn emits $400\text{ Hz}$. The car approaches at $68\text{ m/s}$. What frequency is heard? ($v = 340\text{ m/s}$)
A radar gun emits radio waves at a known frequency. The reflected waves from a moving car return with a slightly different frequency. This frequency difference is used to calculate the car's:
questions.explanation: Police radar uses the Doppler effect — the frequency shift between emitted and reflected radio waves reveals the vehicle's speed.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered Doppler effect calculations!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the formulas for source moving vs. observer moving.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on $f' = f \times v/(v \pm v_s)$ and when to use + vs. −.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: approaching → higher frequency, receding → lower frequency, and the Doppler formula.
